steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the model predicted about P1 and P2's impressionist ratings, given that P1 and P2 actually gave equal ratings. The key is understanding what different correlation values tell us: the model correlated at 0.58 with P1 but only 0.1 with P2. If the model made the same predictions for both, their correlations would have to be equal. Since they differ sharply, the model must have predicted different ratings for P1 and P2.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'P1's and P2's ratings for impressionist paintings would equal one another.': If the model predicted equal ratings, the correlations with P1 and P2 would be identical — not 0.58 vs. 0.1. This restates the given assumption, not what the model predicted. ✗
  • Choice B — 'P1's and P2's ratings for impressionist paintings would differ from one another.': The divergent correlations (0.58 vs. 0.1) can only occur if the model produced different predicted values for each participant. This is the only explanation consistent with the data. ✓
  • Choice C — 'P1 would derive more aesthetic pleasure from impressionist paintings than from cubist paintings.': A single correlation value between the model and one participant cannot reveal how that participant rates one style relative to another. No within-participant style comparison is possible from this data alone. ✗
  • Choice D — 'P1 would derive less aesthetic pleasure from impressionist paintings than from cubist paintings.': Same problem as C — cross-style comparisons for one participant cannot be derived from correlation values. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
